summary: Jeff Atwood highlights James Shore's nineteen-week change diary, which documents Shore's attempt to introduce agile practices while working as a regular developer with no formal authority. Shore, an experienced XP coach forced to take contract work during the dot-com bust, found himself on a decidedly non-agile team. The diary chronicles his efforts to effect organizational change from the bottom up. Atwood frames it as a compelling case study in grassroots change, noting the bittersweet mix of successes and failures while affirming that individual developers can make a difference.
key_points: Organizational change can be attempted without formal power or authority
Even experienced coaches and consultants sometimes end up as regular team members
Bottom-up change efforts yield mixed results—both successes and failures
Individual developers can make a meaningful difference in their organizations
money_quotes: As a peon, could I make the kinds of changes I made as a (damned good!) XP coach?
He was attempting to effect organizational change with no formal power.
It's a bittersweet story of success and failure, but it does show that one developer can make a difference.
key_insight: Individual developers without formal authority can still drive meaningful organizational change, even if the results are mixed.
